claims.claim_text: The opening fintech outage story is presented as a real September 2023 incident with specific architecture, outage duration, and $2.6 million impact.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: In September 2023, a fintech company running their payment processing platform on Azure Kubernetes Service experienced a cascading failure that brought down all customer-facing services for nearly four hours. The root cause was not a bug in their application code. It was an architecture decision made months earlier: every workload, from critical payment APIs to batch reporting jobs, ran in a single system node pool on three Standard_D4s_v3 instances. When Azure initiated a routine host maintenance event on one of the underlying physical servers, the node drained its pods. The remaining two nodes could not absorb the spike, and Kubernetes started evicting pods with the lowest priority---which happened to include their payment reconciliation service. The reconciliation service held database locks that the payment API depended on, and within minutes the entire cluster was in a death spiral. The four-hour outage cost the company an estimated $2.6 million in lost transactions and regulatory penalties.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A poorly isolated AKS cluster can fail catastrophically during routine maintenance if critical and non-critical workloads all compete for the same limited node capacity.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with precise dates, architecture details, duration, and financial impact.

claims.claim_text: AKS manages the control-plane components, including the API server, etcd, controller manager, and scheduler.
claims.span_hint: section: The AKS Control Plane
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS core concepts documentation explicitly describes the managed control-plane components.

claims.claim_text: The module says you never pay separately for the control plane because it is free under the current pricing model.
claims.span_hint: section: The AKS Control Plane
claims.anchor_text: You never see these components directly, you never SSH into them, and you never pay separately for them (the control plane is free as of the current pricing model).
claims.suggested_rewrite: You don't manage or SSH into these components directly; Microsoft operates them as part of the AKS service, and cluster-management pricing depends on the AKS tier you choose.
claims.rationale: Current AKS pricing distinguishes between Free, Standard, and Premium cluster-management tiers, so the blanket pricing statement is inaccurate.

claims.claim_text: AKS Standard/Premium include API-server uptime SLA, with 99.95% when using availability zones, 99.9% without zones, and no SLA on Free tier.
claims.span_hint: section: The AKS Control Plane, SLA paragraph
claims.rationale: The AKS pricing-tier documentation gives these SLA conditions directly.

claims.claim_text: The module says Premium tier adds a 99.99% SLA for mission-critical workloads.
claims.span_hint: section: The AKS Control Plane, SLA paragraph
claims.anchor_text: The Premium tier adds a 99.99% SLA for mission-critical workloads.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Premium tier is the long-term-support-oriented AKS tier for workloads that need extended version support.
claims.rationale: The sourced AKS pricing-tier material supports tier differences and uptime-SLA framing, but not this exact Premium-tier number as written here.

claims.claim_text: AKS clusters require at least one system node pool, and system pools are for critical system pods while user pools isolate application workloads.
claims.span_hint: section: System Pools vs User Pools
claims.rationale: The AKS system node pool guidance documents the required system-pool presence and the intended split between system and user pools.

claims.claim_text: The module says system pools automatically receive the CriticalAddonsOnly taint.
claims.span_hint: section: Taints and Tolerations
claims.anchor_text: System pools automatically receive the taint `CriticalAddonsOnly=true:NoSchedule`.
claims.suggested_rewrite: To keep application pods off system pools, add the `CriticalAddonsOnly=true:NoSchedule` taint to a dedicated system node pool.
claims.rationale: AKS automatically labels system pools, but the taint is documented as an enforcement step you apply for dedicated system isolation.

claims.claim_text: The module says every AKS node pool is backed by a Virtual Machine Scale Set.
claims.span_hint: section: Virtual Machine Scale Sets
claims.anchor_text: Every AKS node pool is backed by an Azure Virtual Machine Scale Set (VMSS).
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many AKS node pools are backed by Azure Virtual Machine Scale Sets (VMSS), but AKS also supports Virtual Machines node pools.
claims.rationale: AKS now supports a separate Virtual Machines node-pool type, so the universal VMSS statement is outdated.

claims.claim_text: AKS creates a second node resource group for cluster infrastructure, and direct customizations there do not persist.
claims.span_hint: section: The Infrastructure Resource Group (MC_*)
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS guidance says agent nodes live in a custom MC_* resource group and direct IaaS customizations there do not persist.

claims.claim_text: The module says an Azure region typically contains three availability zones.
claims.span_hint: section: Availability Zones
claims.anchor_text: An Azure region typically contains three availability zones.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many Azure regions offer multiple availability zones.
claims.rationale: The specific count is broader than the sourced AKS availability-zone material used here, while the general multi-zone lesson is still valid.

claims.claim_text: The module says AKS does not guarantee even distribution across zones, gives a 3-1-1 example for five nodes, and recommends always using multiples of three.
claims.span_hint: section: Availability Zones, balance paragraph
claims.anchor_text: There is a catch that trips up many teams: **AKS does not guarantee even distribution across zones**. If you request 5 nodes across 3 zones, you might get 2-2-1 or even 3-1-1 distribution. The VMSS tries to balance, but it is not guaranteed. You should always deploy node counts that are multiples of your zone count (3, 6, 9, 12) to maximize balance.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Zone-spanning node pools aim to stay balanced across selected zones, typically within one node per zone, but temporary imbalances can still happen during failures or scaling events. Verify actual placement rather than assuming perfect spread.
claims.rationale: Current VMSS zone-balancing guidance is stronger and more nuanced than the module's blanket warning and 3-1-1 example.

claims.claim_text: A zonal Azure Disk cannot be used from the wrong zone, so a pod can remain Pending if rescheduled onto a node in another availability zone.
claims.span_hint: section: Availability Zones, persistent volumes paragraph
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS troubleshooting guidance explicitly documents disk/node zone mismatch as a cause of Pending or mount failures and recommends zone-aware placement.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes minor versions release roughly every four months, and AKS supports three GA minor versions at a time.
claims.span_hint: section: Upgrade Channels
claims.rationale: The AKS supported-versions page states both the upstream release cadence and AKS's three-GA-minor support window.

claims.claim_text: The module's upgrade-channel table defines the behaviors of none, patch, stable, rapid, and node-image.
claims.span_hint: section: Upgrade Channels, table
claims.rationale: AKS's automatic-upgrade documentation defines these channels and their version-selection behavior.

claims.claim_text: AKS rolling upgrades add surge nodes, cordon and drain old nodes, and Microsoft recommends 33% max surge for production node pools.
claims.span_hint: section: How AKS Upgrades Work Under the Hood
claims.rationale: The AKS rolling-upgrade documentation describes this flow and explicitly recommends 33% max surge for production pools.

claims.claim_text: The module says AKS nodes use managed Premium SSD OS disks by default.
claims.span_hint: section: Ephemeral OS Disks, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: By default, AKS nodes use managed OS disks backed by Azure Premium SSD.
claims.suggested_rewrite: AKS can use managed OS disks or ephemeral OS disks, and it defaults to ephemeral OS disks when the node-pool configuration supports them and you don't explicitly request managed disks.
claims.rationale: Current AKS storage guidance says ephemeral OS disks are now the default when possible, so the blanket managed-disk default is outdated.

claims.claim_text: Ephemeral OS disks use local host storage, lower OS-disk latency, and speed up node scaling or cluster upgrades.
claims.span_hint: section: Ephemeral OS Disks, overview bullets
claims.rationale: AKS storage guidance explicitly documents these performance and operational benefits of ephemeral OS disks.

claims.claim_text: The module gives exact ephemeral-OS startup timings of 20-30 seconds versus 60-90 seconds.
claims.span_hint: section: Ephemeral OS Disks, bullet list
claims.anchor_text: - **Faster node startup**: No need to create and attach a remote disk. Nodes boot in roughly 20-30 seconds instead of 60-90 seconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Faster node startup**: Because the OS disk stays on local storage, nodes can start faster than comparable managed-OS-disk configurations.
claims.rationale: The exact timing numbers are environment-dependent and not backed by an authoritative source here.

claims.claim_text: The module says ephemeral OS disks are reimaged on every reboot.
claims.span_hint: section: Ephemeral OS Disks, bullet list
claims.anchor_text: - **Reimaged on every reboot**: If a node is deallocated and reallocated, it gets a fresh OS image.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Recreated on reimage or deallocate/reallocate**: If a node using an ephemeral OS disk is reimaged or deallocated and reallocated, its OS state is reprovisioned from a fresh image.
claims.rationale: The lifecycle event described is deallocate/reallocate or reimage, not every reboot.

claims.claim_text: The module says Standard_D4s_v5 supports ephemeral OS disks through a 100 GB cache example.
claims.span_hint: section: Ephemeral OS Disks, VM-size paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Not all VM sizes support ephemeral OS disks. The VM's temporary storage or cache must be large enough to hold the OS image (typically 128 GB). Standard_D4s_v5 has a 100 GB cache, which works with the default 128 GB OS disk size if you set `--os-disk-size-gb 100` or use a VM with larger cache. Check the [VM sizes documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/sizes) to confirm support.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Not all VM sizes support ephemeral OS disks. The VM's local cache, temp disk, or NVMe storage must be large enough for the OS image, so you should confirm support against the current VM-size documentation before choosing a SKU.
claims.rationale: Current Dsv5 size documentation says Standard_D4s_v5 has no local storage and does not support ephemeral OS disks.

claims.claim_text: The module frames the default AKS access model as certificate-based kubeconfig auth and says certificates lack reasonable expiry, revocation, and auditability.
claims.span_hint: section: Entra ID Integration, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: The default AKS authentication mechanism uses x509 client certificates embedded in the kubeconfig file. This is terrible for production. Certificates do not expire on a reasonable schedule, cannot be individually revoked without rotating the entire cluster CA, and provide no audit trail of who did what.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For production, prefer Microsoft Entra integration for user authentication and consider disabling local accounts if you want to remove static `--admin` credentials. That gives you token-based user access with better central identity governance and auditing than relying on local cluster credentials.
claims.rationale: AKS local-account behavior is more nuanced than the module states, and Entra integration does not by itself remove all static admin access unless local accounts are disabled.

claims.claim_text: With Microsoft Entra integration, users authenticate to AKS using Microsoft Entra tokens and access can be based on user or group membership.
claims.span_hint: section: Entra ID Integration, auth-flow prose
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS Entra/RBAC documentation explicitly describes token-based sign-in and authorization based on identity or group membership.

claims.claim_text: Legacy Azure AD integration is deprecated and AKS-managed Microsoft Entra integration is the current approach.
claims.span_hint: section: Setting Up Entra ID Integration
claims.rationale: Microsoft's legacy integration page marks the old model deprecated and points users to the AKS-managed experience.

claims.claim_text: Azure RBAC for AKS provides four built-in roles: Cluster Admin, Admin, Writer, and Reader.
claims.span_hint: section: Entra ID Integration, Azure RBAC role table
claims.rationale: The Azure RBAC for Kubernetes Authorization documentation lists these built-in AKS roles and their scope semantics.

claims.claim_text: The Did You Know item hard-codes Standard-tier pricing at $0.10 per cluster-hour as of 2025.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.anchor_text: 1. **AKS control plane costs nothing.** Microsoft does not charge for the Kubernetes control plane. You only pay for the VMs (nodes) in your cluster and any associated resources like load balancers and disks. The Standard tier ($0.10/cluster-hour as of 2025) adds the SLA guarantee and advanced features like long-term support versions, but the core control plane itself remains free.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **AKS cluster-management pricing depends on the tier.** Free only charges for underlying resources, while Standard and Premium add cluster-management charges and SLA-backed features.
claims.rationale: Exact cloud pricing is time- and offer-dependent, and the broader statement also collapses AKS tier pricing too aggressively.

claims.claim_text: The autogenerated MC_ node resource group name has an 80-character limit.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 2
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS naming FAQ explicitly documents the 80-character limit for autogenerated MC_ resource group names.

claims.claim_text: The module says Azure Linux became the default OS for new AKS clusters since late 2023, replacing Ubuntu.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.anchor_text: 3. **AKS nodes run a Microsoft-maintained Linux distribution called Azure Linux (formerly CBL-Mariner).** Since late 2023, Azure Linux has been the default OS for new AKS clusters, replacing Ubuntu. Azure Linux is optimized for container workloads with a smaller attack surface, faster boot times, and automatic security patches through the node image upgrade mechanism.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **AKS supports Azure Linux for Linux node pools.** Azure Linux is Microsoft's container-focused Linux distribution for AKS, but Ubuntu remains the default Linux distro on AKS unless you choose the Azure Linux OS SKU.
claims.rationale: Current AKS core-concepts documentation says Ubuntu is still the default Linux distro on AKS, so the replacement claim is inaccurate.

claims.claim_text: The cluster autoscaler defaults cited here are a 10-second scan interval, 10-minute unneeded time, and 50% utilization threshold for scale-down candidacy.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS autoscaling training material documents these default cluster-autoscaler profile values.

further_reading.title: Core concepts for Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
further_reading.why_relevant: Best current overview for control plane, node pools, node resource groups, OS choices, and AKS operating modes.

further_reading.title: Configure availability zones in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains zone-spanning versus regional behavior, zone resilience, and zone-aware workload placement in AKS.

further_reading.title: Patch and upgrade Azure Kubernetes Service worker nodes and Kubernetes versions
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers current Microsoft guidance for upgrade channels, node-image strategy, maintenance windows, and production-safe rollout patterns.

notes: Mermaid diagrams, code blocks, quiz items, and hands-on exercise steps were intentionally excluded per the task rules. Repeated overview claims were covered at their most sourceable narrative locations rather than duplicating identical citations for every later restatement.
